state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
product_description: Enterprise workflow automation platform with visual flow builder, 200+ app connectors, conditional logic, error handling, and execution monitoring.

plan.description: Define business requirements, user personas, core use cases, and ROI model for the enterprise workflow automation platform. Document process flows for key user journeys: flow creation, connector setup, monitoring, and error resolution.
acceptance_criteria: BRD covers at minimum: flow builder, connector library, conditional logic, error handling, monitoring
At least 3 distinct user personas documented with goals and pain points
ROI model includes time-saved metrics vs. manual workflow execution
Process flows validated against 5+ real enterprise workflow patterns
Integration points with existing systems mapped
Migration plan drafted

plan.description: Produce the Product Requirements Document, MVP vs. full roadmap, feature prioritization matrix (MoSCoW), and success metrics (DAU, flows executed/day, connector adoption rate, MTTR for failed flows).
acceptance_criteria: PRD covers all features: flow builder, 200+ connectors, conditional logic, error handling, execution monitoring
MVP scope clearly delineated with justification
Success metrics are quantifiable and have baseline targets
Roadmap approved by product stakeholders

plan.description: Competitive analysis of Zapier, Make, n8n, Workato, and Tray.io. Identify differentiation opportunities in enterprise segment: pricing, connector depth, on-premise support, compliance posture.
acceptance_criteria: Feature comparison matrix covers all 5 competitors across 10+ dimensions
Identified at least 3 clear differentiation opportunities
Pricing analysis includes per-seat, consumption-based, and flat-rate models
Positioning statement drafted based on findings

plan.description: Draft Terms of Service, Privacy Policy, Data Processing Agreement (DPA), connector vendor licensing review, and open-source dependency license audit.
acceptance_criteria: ToS and Privacy Policy reviewed by legal counsel
DPA template covers EU/US data residency requirements
OSS license audit flags any GPL/AGPL dependencies in commercial distribution
Connector usage rights confirmed for top-50 connectors at minimum

plan.description: Produce threat model (STRIDE), define security architecture for credential vault (connector OAuth tokens, API keys), tenant isolation model, RBAC design, and penetration test plan. Map controls to ISO 27001 Annex A and SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria.
acceptance_criteria: STRIDE threat model covers all 5 system components
Credential vault design uses envelope encryption (AES-256 + KMS)
RBAC matrix covers: admin, flow_builder, viewer, connector_manager roles
SOC 2 CC6.x and CC7.x controls mapped to implementation artifacts
Pen test plan scoped and signed off

plan.description: Design the visual flow builder canvas (drag-and-drop nodes, edge connectors, zoom/pan), connector picker, conditional logic editor (branch/merge/loop nodes), execution history timeline, and error detail panel. Produce wireframes and a clickable Figma prototype.
acceptance_criteria: All 7 screens have high-fidelity wireframes
Clickable Figma prototype covers the primary flow: create flow → add connector → add condition → save → monitor
Usability test with 5 target users — task completion rate ≥ 80% for 'create a 3-step flow'
WCAG 2.1 AA checklist completed for all screens

plan.description: Design the multi-tenant database schema: flows, steps, connectors, credentials (encrypted), executions, execution_logs, schedules, teams, and audit_events. Include migration scripts, indexing strategy for execution queries, and data retention policies.
acceptance_criteria: ERD covers all entities with FK relationships
Row-level security policies enforce tenant isolation — verified by cross-tenant query test
Credential columns use pgcrypto or equivalent column-level encryption
Execution log table has composite index on (tenant_id, flow_id, started_at)
Migration scripts are idempotent and tested on clean schema

plan.description: Design the OpenAPI 3.1 contract for all platform endpoints: flow CRUD, step management, connector registry, credential vault, execution trigger and status, schedule management, monitoring/metrics, and webhook ingress.
acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI spec passes linting with zero errors (Spectral ruleset)
All endpoints include request/response schemas with examples
Auth flows documented for both OAuth2 and API key paths
Breaking-change policy documented in spec header

plan.description: Set up the monorepo structure (backend, frontend, connectors, infra), language toolchains (Python/FastAPI for execution engine, TypeScript/React for UI, Go for connector runtime), environment config management (per-environment secrets via Vault/AWS SSM), and local dev bootstrap script.
acceptance_criteria: `make bootstrap` produces a running local environment in under 5 minutes
All services start via `docker-compose up` with zero manual steps
Secrets loaded from Vault — no plaintext credentials in any config file
Monorepo lint, type-check, and test commands all pass on clean checkout

plan.description: Implement the core execution engine: DAG-based flow runner, step scheduler, retry/backoff with configurable policies, dead-letter queue for permanently failed steps, and parallel branch executor. Use Multi-Agent Parallel Execution pattern for independent branches.
acceptance_criteria: Engine executes a 10-step linear flow end-to-end with all step outputs persisted
Parallel branches execute concurrently — measured with timing assertions
Retry with exponential backoff triggers on transient connector errors (HTTP 429, 503)
Dead-letter queue captures steps that exhaust retries — payload preserved for replay
Execution context is isolated per tenant (no cross-tenant state leak)

plan.description: Build the conditional logic engine: expression evaluator (JSON Logic or CEL), branch resolver, loop constructs (for-each, while-condition), merge/join gates, and filter nodes. Support data-mapping with JSONPath/JMESPath transforms.
acceptance_criteria: If/else branch correctly routes execution based on previous step output
For-each loop processes each item in an array as independent sub-execution
Expression evaluator rejects unsafe expressions (no arbitrary code execution)
Transform node maps nested JSON structures using JMESPath — 10 test cases pass
Merge/join gate waits for all parallel branches before continuing

plan.description: Implement the connector framework: base connector interface, OAuth2/API key auth handlers, connector configuration schema (JSON Schema), request/response normalisation, rate-limit aware throttling, and the connector SDK for third-party developers.
acceptance_criteria: Base connector interface enforces execute(), validate_config(), and get_auth_url() methods
OAuth2 PKCE flow completes end-to-end for a test connector
Rate limiter throttles requests when connector limit is reached — verified with load test
Connector SDK docs include a 'hello world' connector buildable in under 30 minutes
Config schema validation rejects malformed connector configs with descriptive errors

plan.description: Build the first 20 high-priority connectors (Slack, Gmail, GitHub, Salesforce, HubSpot, Jira, Google Sheets, Dropbox, S3, PostgreSQL, MySQL, HTTP/Webhook, Stripe, Twilio, SendGrid, Notion, Airtable, Zendesk, Microsoft Teams, ServiceNow) using the connector framework.
acceptance_criteria: All 20 connectors implement the base connector interface
Each connector has at minimum 3 actions and 1 trigger
Integration tests run against sandbox/test accounts for each connector
Credential storage verified: plaintext secrets never logged or stored unencrypted
Each connector has a config schema with field-level descriptions

plan.description: Build out connectors 21–200+: batch implementation of remaining connectors grouped by category (CRM, ITSM, storage, communication, databases, payments, analytics, HR, marketing, DevOps). Automate connector scaffolding from OpenAPI spec ingestion.
acceptance_criteria: 200+ connectors registered in the connector registry
Auto-scaffolding generates 80%+ of boilerplate from a valid OpenAPI spec
Each connector passes the connector contract test suite (auth, execute, error_handling)
Connector registry searchable by name, category, and auth type
Zero hardcoded credentials in any connector implementation

plan.description: Implement the error handling subsystem: error classification (transient vs. permanent), configurable error handlers per step (retry, skip, fail-flow, branch-to-error-handler), error notification routing (email/Slack/webhook), and flow-level error recovery strategies.
acceptance_criteria: Error classification correctly categorises 10 test error scenarios
Per-step error handler config is persisted and applied at execution time
Notification fires within 30 seconds of a permanent flow failure
Manual replay from a specific step replays only remaining steps — prior outputs reused
Error branch routing successfully diverts flow to an alternate path on step failure

plan.description: Build the execution monitoring service: real-time execution state machine, step-level timing and output capture, execution history store with search/filter, metrics aggregation (success rate, P95 latency, error rate per flow/connector), and streaming execution log via SSE.

payload.state_machine: execution_states: pending queued running paused failed succeeded

acceptance_criteria: Execution state transitions are atomic and persisted — no lost state on crash
SSE stream delivers step-completion events within 500ms of actual completion
Metrics dashboard shows success rate, P95 latency, and error rate per flow
Execution history search returns results in under 200ms for 1M+ log entries
Execution logs retained per configured policy — older entries auto-archived

plan.description: Implement the schedule engine: cron-based triggers, interval triggers, event/webhook triggers, timezone-aware scheduling, schedule conflict detection, and schedule health monitoring (missed run detection).
acceptance_criteria: Cron trigger fires within 5 seconds of scheduled time under normal load
Timezone-aware scheduling correctly handles DST transitions
Missed run detection alerts operator within 5 minutes of a missed schedule
Webhook trigger validates HMAC signature before enqueuing execution
Schedule conflict detection warns when two schedules would fire the same flow simultaneously

plan.description: Build the visual flow builder canvas: React Flow-based drag-and-drop editor, node palette, connector search, node configuration side panel, canvas zoom/pan/minimap, flow version history viewer, undo/redo, and keyboard shortcuts.
acceptance_criteria: Flow canvas renders 100-node flows without visible lag (< 16ms frame time)
Drag-and-drop from palette to canvas creates a configured node
Undo/redo works across 20 sequential operations
Connector search returns results as user types (debounced, < 100ms)
Auto-layout arranges nodes into a readable DAG on demand
Canvas state auto-saves every 30 seconds

plan.description: Build the execution monitoring dashboard: live execution list with status badges, step-by-step execution trace viewer, real-time log stream panel (SSE), execution metrics charts (success rate, duration histogram, error breakdown), and flow health scorecard.
acceptance_criteria: Execution list updates in real-time as new executions start/complete
Execution trace shows each step's status, duration, input, and output
Live log panel streams logs without page reload
Metrics charts refresh every 30 seconds without full re-render
Flow health scorecard shows 7-day success rate and trend

plan.description: Build the connector marketplace UI: browsable connector catalog with category filters, connector detail page (actions, triggers, auth setup, changelog), credential manager, and connector usage analytics per tenant.
acceptance_criteria: Catalog displays 200+ connectors with category filter and search
Connector detail page shows all available actions and triggers with parameter descriptions
Credential setup wizard completes OAuth2 flow in-browser without redirect issues
Usage analytics shows which connectors are used most in tenant's flows
Connector catalog loads in under 1 second (client-side search with preloaded index)

plan.description: Build team management, RBAC UI, audit log viewer, usage/billing dashboard, and tenant settings panel. Implement SSO configuration page (SAML 2.0 / OIDC).
acceptance_criteria: Admin can create/edit/delete roles and assign to users
Audit log is searchable and filterable by event type, user, and date range
Usage dashboard shows API calls, executions, and connector usage for billing period
SSO configuration wizard validates SAML metadata before saving
Role changes take effect on next request — no stale permissions cached > 60 seconds

plan.description: Implement the AI Flow Assistant using the Review and Critique (Actor-Critic) pattern: an LLM-powered assistant that suggests next steps in a flow, detects common misconfiguration patterns, and critiques drafted flows for error-handling gaps and performance anti-patterns.
acceptance_criteria: Flow assistant suggests relevant next steps based on current flow context in < 2 seconds
Critic agent identifies missing error handlers on HTTP action nodes
Suggestion quality score ≥ 0.8 on a held-out test set of 50 real flow patterns
HITL gate: suggestions are proposals — user must explicitly accept before applying to flow
No hallucinated connector names or actions in suggestions

plan.description: Produce ISO 27001 Statement of Applicability (SoA), SOC 2 Type II readiness assessment, information security policies (ISMS), data classification policy, incident response plan, and vulnerability management policy.
acceptance_criteria: SoA documents all 93 ISO 27001 Annex A controls with in-scope/out-of-scope justification
SOC 2 readiness gap assessment lists all open items with owners and due dates
Incident response plan tested via tabletop exercise — debrief report produced
Data classification policy covers all data types in the platform schema
Audit log export covers all SOC 2 CC6.x control evidence requirements

plan.description: Build CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions): lint → unit test → integration test → build → SAST/DAST scan → staging deploy → smoke test → production deploy with canary rollout. Set up observability stack (Prometheus + Grafana + Loki + OpenTelemetry), alerting runbooks, and SLO dashboards.
acceptance_criteria: Full pipeline completes in under 15 minutes for a typical PR
SAST scan blocks merge on high/critical findings
Canary deployment routes 10% traffic before full promotion
SLO dashboards show error budget burn rate with alerting at 5% burn
Rollback pipeline executes in under 3 minutes

plan.description: Provision production infrastructure via Terraform: multi-AZ Kubernetes cluster, managed PostgreSQL + TimescaleDB, Redis (task queue + cache), Elasticsearch, Vault (credential store), CDN, WAF, and VPC network segmentation. Define IaC modules for dev/staging/prod parity.
acceptance_criteria: `terraform apply` provisions a complete environment from scratch with zero manual steps
Multi-AZ failover tested: primary DB failure results in < 30s failover
WAF rules block OWASP Top 10 attack patterns — verified with automated scan
Dev/staging/prod use identical module configs — only sizing parameters differ
Vault unsealing is automated via AWS KMS auto-unseal

plan.description: Design the master test plan: unit test strategy (≥ 80% coverage), integration test strategy (connector contract tests, API contract tests), performance test plan (execution engine under load), security test plan, and regression suite coverage map.
acceptance_criteria: Master test plan approved covering all 6 test levels
Coverage targets documented and enforced in CI pipeline
Test environment provisioning script creates isolated test data per run
Defect triage process defined: severity levels, SLA per severity, escalation path
Regression suite covers all P0 user journeys

plan.description: Write unit tests for the execution engine, conditional logic engine, connector framework, error handler, and schedule engine. Achieve ≥ 80% line coverage. Include property-based tests for the expression evaluator.
acceptance_criteria: Overall unit test coverage ≥ 80% (enforced in CI)
Execution engine: all state transitions covered
Expression evaluator: 100 property-based test cases with no failures
Connector framework: auth flows, rate limiting, and error classification all tested
All tests pass in < 2 minutes in CI

plan.description: Build end-to-end system test suite: 20 golden-path flow scenarios spanning multiple connectors and conditional logic, multi-tenant isolation validation, execution monitoring verification, schedule trigger accuracy tests, and load test (1000 concurrent executions).
acceptance_criteria: All 20 golden-path scenarios pass in staging environment
Cross-tenant isolation test: 0 data leaks across 10 tenant boundary test cases
Load test: P95 execution start latency < 500ms at 1000 concurrent executions
Schedule accuracy: 100 consecutive cron triggers fire within 5 seconds of scheduled time
Error recovery scenario: flow resumes correctly after simulated connector outage
Load test simulates expected concurrent users
Failover scenario tested

plan.description: Write SLA definitions (99.9% uptime, < 5s P95 execution latency), operational runbooks (incident response, on-call escalation, flow execution stuck recovery, connector auth refresh, DB failover), capacity planning model, and disaster recovery plan.
acceptance_criteria: SLA document signed off with measurable SLOs and error budgets
Each runbook has a step-by-step procedure testable via tabletop
DR plan tested: restore from backup completes within 4-hour RTO
Capacity model covers 3x current peak load with headroom calculations
On-call rotation and escalation path documented and communicated

plan.description: Produce user-facing documentation: Getting Started guide, visual flow builder tutorial, connector setup guides for top-20 connectors, conditional logic cookbook, error handling best practices, API reference, and 5 video walkthroughs.
acceptance_criteria: Getting Started guide takes a new user from signup to first running flow in under 15 minutes
All 20 tier-1 connector setup guides reviewed for accuracy against live connectors
API reference auto-generated from OpenAPI spec and kept in sync via CI
5 video walkthroughs cover: create flow, add connector, conditional logic, error handling, monitoring
In-app tooltips cover all node configuration fields
Admin guide separate from end-user guide
Role-based training paths defined

plan.description: Build the pricing model: consumption-based (executions/month), per-seat, and enterprise flat-rate tiers. Model unit economics (infra cost per 1M executions, connector API cost pass-through), break-even analysis, and billing integration design (Stripe).
acceptance_criteria: Three-tier pricing model with clear feature gates between tiers
Unit economics model shows cost per 1M executions at 3 scale levels
Break-even analysis at each pricing tier documented
Stripe billing integration handles subscription creation, metered usage, and invoicing
Usage metering captures execution count per tenant per billing period

plan.description: Set up i18n infrastructure for the React frontend (i18next), extract all UI strings to translation keys, and produce initial translations for English, Spanish, French, German, and Japanese.
acceptance_criteria: Zero hardcoded UI strings — all text rendered via i18next keys
Locale switching works at runtime without page reload
All 5 locales have 100% string coverage (no fallback to English keys in non-English mode)
CI fails if a new hardcoded string is introduced (automated string extraction check)
Date, number, and currency formatting uses locale-aware formatters

flaws: Step 25 (Infra) depends on Step 24 (CI/CD) — this is backwards. You cannot run a CI/CD pipeline that deploys to infrastructure that doesn't exist yet. Terraform provisioning must precede pipeline construction, not follow it.
Step 28 (System Tests) lists depends_on [14,15,16,17,24] but omits Step 25 (Infra). System tests in staging require production-equivalent infrastructure to be provisioned first.

The Celery vs Temporal decision is punted with 'or' syntax in steps 10 and 17. These are architecturally incompatible execution models with different failure semantics, replay guarantees, and operational overhead. Leaving this unresolved creates a fork in the entire execution engine implementation.

Step 14's 200+ connector target is catastrophically underscoped as a single task. Salesforce alone requires navigating 4 OAuth flows, 2 API versions, governor limits, and a SOAP fallback. ServiceNow requires tenant-specific instance URLs. Microsoft Graph requires multi-tenant Azure app registration. Auto-scaffolding from OpenAPI specs covers maybe 30% of real connector complexity — the '80% boilerplate' claim is fiction for enterprise APIs.

Step 23 (Compliance — ISO 27001 SoA, SOC 2 readiness) depends only on Steps 5 and 7. It should depend on steps 10-21 since you cannot write an accurate Statement of Applicability for a system that hasn't been built yet. The SoA documents controls as-implemented, not as-designed.

No step for connector versioning or API change management. When Salesforce deprecates an API version or GitHub changes a webhook payload schema, every flow using that connector silently breaks. This is the #1 operational failure mode for platforms like this and it has zero coverage in the plan.

Billing infrastructure (Step 31) comes after system tests (Step 28). If metered billing is a product requirement — and it is, given the consumption-based tier — usage metering must be instrumented in the execution engine (Step 10) from day one. Retrofitting metering into a built execution engine is a significant rework.

The plan mentions on-premise deployment as a competitive differentiator (Step 3) but contains zero implementation steps for Helm charts, air-gapped Vault setup, or self-hosted connector registry. This is a promised feature with no delivery path.

React Flow 100-node canvas at <16ms frame time is achievable in isolation but the plan adds undo/redo (Zustand), auto-save every 30s, and real-time collaboration state. No performance budget is allocated for the combined load. React Flow has documented edge virtualization issues above ~150 nodes with complex edge routing.
suggestions: Resolve Celery vs Temporal before Step 9. Run a spike (2-3 days): implement a 3-step flow with error handling and replay in both, measure operational complexity. Temporal wins on durability guarantees; Celery wins on simplicity. Pick one and delete the other from the plan.

Split Step 14 into at minimum 5 sub-tasks with dedicated owners per API category. Assign a 'connector contract test harness' sub-task first — a standard test fixture that validates auth, pagination, rate limit handling, and error normalization for any connector. Build all 200+ against it, not after it.

Flip the dependency: Step 25 (Infra) should be Step 24, and current Step 24 (CI/CD pipelines) should be Step 25. Alternatively, split infra into 'minimal dev infra' (precedes CI/CD) and 'production infra' (follows CI/CD pipeline validation).

Add an explicit 'connector health monitoring' step: a background job that pings each connector's auth endpoint on a schedule, detects API schema drift by diffing against the stored JSON Schema, and alerts when a connector's error rate spikes. Without this, 200+ connectors become a silent maintenance liability.

Add execution payload encryption as a distinct database requirement. Credentials are encrypted (Step 7), but flow execution payloads can contain PII from connector responses (email bodies, CRM records, payment data). Column-level encryption or application-level encryption of step_outputs is required for GDPR Article 32 compliance.

Move billing metering instrumentation to Step 10 (execution engine). Add a 'emit metering event' call at execution completion. The Step 31 Stripe integration can come later, but the metering data must be captured from execution #1 or you have no historical billing data to reconstruct.

Add an SSRF protection layer to the HTTP/Webhook connector and any connector that accepts user-supplied URLs. Without it, any tenant can use the HTTP connector to probe your internal VPC: hitting Vault's API, the metadata endpoint (169.254.169.254), or internal Kubernetes service IPs.
missing: Connector versioning strategy: how do you handle breaking changes in an upstream API without invalidating every flow that uses that connector? Semantic versioning for connectors + migration guide per breaking change is standard practice and entirely absent.

OAuth token refresh concurrency control: when 10 parallel flow executions hit a connector whose token has expired, all 10 will attempt a token refresh simultaneously, causing a thundering herd against the connector's token endpoint. A distributed lock per credential is required.

Data residency implementation step: GDPR and enterprise buyers require EU data to stay in EU. The plan mentions this in legal (Step 4) and infra (Step 25) but has no implementation step for geo-routing execution data, per-tenant region selection, or cross-region replication controls.

Webhook replay attack mitigation: Step 17 requires HMAC signature validation but does not require timestamp validation or nonce tracking. Without replay protection, an attacker who captures a valid signed webhook payload can trigger arbitrary flow executions indefinitely.

Database connection pooling (PgBouncer or pgpool): at 1000 concurrent executions (Step 28 load target), each with 3-5 DB operations, PostgreSQL will be saturated without a connection pooler. This is an infrastructure component missing from Step 25.

Flow import/export and portability: enterprise buyers require the ability to export flows as JSON/YAML for version control, disaster recovery, and environment promotion (staging → production). Absent from all 32 steps.

Connector SDK versioning and backward compatibility policy: third-party connector developers (Step 12) need a stable interface contract with a deprecation timeline. Without this, every framework change breaks third-party connectors.

Step-level timeout configuration: a connector that hangs indefinitely will block a Celery worker or Temporal workflow indefinitely. Per-step timeouts with configurable defaults are absent from the execution engine spec.

Multi-region active-active or active-passive topology decision: the infra step specifies multi-AZ (single region) but enterprise SLAs at 99.9%+ often require multi-region. This architectural decision affects database replication strategy, Vault HA topology, and Kubernetes federation — it must be decided before Step 25.
security_risks: SSRF via HTTP connector: no egress filtering mentioned anywhere. A malicious or misconfigured flow can use the HTTP connector to exfiltrate data from internal services, hit cloud metadata endpoints, or probe Vault's API. Mitigate with an egress proxy that blocks RFC 1918 addresses and cloud metadata IPs.

Expression injection via CEL: Step 11 mandates 'expression eval in isolated context' but CEL's Go implementation has had sandbox escapes, and the Python CEL implementations have varying security postures. If the sandboxing is implemented incorrectly, any flow builder can execute arbitrary code on the execution engine worker.

Connector OAuth token exfiltration: Vault stores tokens per-tenant, but the execution engine must decrypt them at runtime. If execution worker memory is dumped (OOM, core dump, debug endpoint), all in-flight OAuth tokens are exposed. Secure enclave or token proxy pattern is the mitigation.

Cross-tenant timing oracle via execution metrics: even with RLS, if the metrics endpoint exposes P95 latency by connector globally (not per-tenant), an attacker can infer what connectors other tenants are using and roughly when their flows execute.

Connector callback URL hijacking: OAuth flows require a redirect URI. If the callback URL validation is lenient (prefix match instead of exact match), an attacker can redirect another tenant's OAuth completion to their own endpoint and steal the authorization code.

Execution log data retention vs right-to-erasure conflict: execution logs contain step inputs/outputs which may include personal data. The 90-day default retention (Step 16) conflicts with GDPR Article 17 right-to-erasure requests, which must be honored within 30 days. No GDPR erasure implementation step exists.
final_review.summary: This plan demonstrates solid architectural instincts — PostgreSQL + RLS for multi-tenancy, envelope encryption for credentials, DAG-based execution, actor-critic AI assistant — but it fails on scope realism and several critical dependency ordering errors. The 200+ connector target is the plan's most dangerous fiction: real enterprise connectors (Salesforce, SAP, ServiceNow, Microsoft Graph) each represent weeks of engineering, not items in a batch task. The Celery-vs-Temporal non-decision creates a branching path through the most critical subsystem in the product. Three security gaps (SSRF, OAuth token concurrency, webhook replay) are production-blocking for enterprise buyers. The infra/CI-CD dependency inversion would cause a pipeline configuration sprint that has nowhere to deploy to. Stripped to a realistic MVP scope — flow builder, top-20 connectors, core execution engine, basic monitoring, single-region deployment — this is a 68-point plan. As written, presenting 200+ connectors, ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, and multi-region HA as a coherent deliverable sequence, it scores 58: the core is buildable, but the scope promises will cause the project to stall at Step 14 and never recover.

flaws: Step 25 (Infra) depends on Step 24 (CI/CD) — this is backwards. You cannot run a CI/CD pipeline that deploys to infrastructure that doesn't exist yet. Terraform provisioning must precede pipeline construction, not follow it.
Step 28 (System Tests) lists depends_on [14,15,16,17,24] but omits Step 25 (Infra). System tests in staging require production-equivalent infrastructure to be provisioned first.

The Celery vs Temporal decision is punted with 'or' syntax in steps 10 and 17. These are architecturally incompatible execution models with different failure semantics, replay guarantees, and operational overhead. Leaving this unresolved creates a fork in the entire execution engine implementation.

Step 14's 200+ connector target is catastrophically underscoped as a single task. Salesforce alone requires navigating 4 OAuth flows, 2 API versions, governor limits, and a SOAP fallback. ServiceNow requires tenant-specific instance URLs. Microsoft Graph requires multi-tenant Azure app registration. Step 23 (Compliance — ISO 27001 SoA, SOC 2 readiness) depends only on Steps 5 and 7. It should depend on steps 10-21 since you cannot write an accurate Statement of Applicability for a system that hasn't been built yet. The SoA documents controls as-implemented, not as-designed.

No step for connector versioning or API change management. When Salesforce deprecates an API version or GitHub changes a webhook payload schema, every flow using that connector silently breaks. This is the #1 operational failure mode for platforms like this and it has zero coverage in the plan.

Billing infrastructure (Step 31) comes after system tests (Step 28). If metered billing is a product requirement — and it is, given the consumption-based tier — usage metering must be instrumented in the execution engine (Step 10) from day one. Retrofitting metering into a built execution engine is a significant rework.

The plan mentions on-premise deployment as a competitive differentiator (Step 3) but contains zero implementation steps for Helm charts, air-gapped Vault setup, or self-hosted connector registry. This is a promised feature with no delivery path.

React Flow 100-node canvas at <16ms frame time is achievable in isolation but the plan adds undo/redo (Zustand), auto-save every 30s, and real-time collaboration state. No performance budget is allocated for the combined load. React Flow has documented edge virtualization issues above ~150 nodes with complex edge routing.

Step-level timeout configuration: a connector that hangs indefinitely will block a Celery worker or Temporal workflow indefinitely. Per-step timeouts with configurable defaults are absent from the execution engine spec.

Multi-region active-active or active-passive topology decision: the infra step specifies multi-AZ (single region) but enterprise SLAs at 99.9%+ often require multi-region. This architectural decision affects database replication strategy, Vault HA topology, and Kubernetes federation — it must be decided before Step 25.
security_risks: SSRF via HTTP connector: no egress filtering mentioned anywhere. A malicious or misconfigured flow can use the HTTP connector to exfiltrate data from internal services, hit cloud metadata endpoints, or probe Vault's API. Mitigate with an egress proxy that blocks RFC 1918 addresses and cloud metadata IPs.

Expression injection via CEL: Step 11 mandates 'expression eval in isolated context' but CEL's Go implementation has had sandbox escapes, and the Python CEL implementations have varying security postures. If the sandboxing is implemented incorrectly, any flow builder can execute arbitrary code on the execution engine worker.

Connector OAuth token exfiltration: Vault stores tokens per-tenant, but the execution engine must decrypt them at runtime. If execution worker memory is dumped (OOM, core dump, debug endpoint), all in-flight OAuth tokens are exposed. Secure enclave or token proxy pattern is the mitigation.

Cross-tenant timing oracle via execution metrics: even with RLS, if the metrics endpoint exposes P95 latency by connector globally (not per-tenant), an attacker can infer what connectors other tenants are using and roughly when their flows execute.

Connector callback URL hijacking: OAuth flows require a redirect URI. If the callback URL validation is lenient (prefix match instead of exact match), an attacker can redirect another tenant's OAuth completion to their own endpoint and steal the authorization code.

Execution log data retention vs right-to-erasure conflict: execution logs contain step inputs/outputs which may include personal data. The 90-day default retention (Step 16) conflicts with GDPR Article 17 right-to-erasure requests, which must be honored within 30 days. No GDPR erasure implementation step exists.
history.summary: This plan demonstrates solid architectural instincts — PostgreSQL + RLS for multi-tenancy, envelope encryption for credentials, DAG-based execution, actor-critic AI assistant — but it fails on scope realism and several critical dependency ordering errors. The 200+ connector target is the plan's most dangerous fiction: real enterprise connectors (Salesforce, SAP, ServiceNow, Microsoft Graph) each represent weeks of engineering, not items in a batch task. The Celery-vs-Temporal non-decision creates a branching path through the most critical subsystem in the product. Three security gaps (SSRF, OAuth token concurrency, webhook replay) are production-blocking for enterprise buyers. The infra/CI-CD dependency inversion would cause a pipeline configuration sprint that has nowhere to deploy to. Stripped to a realistic MVP scope — flow builder, top-20 connectors, core execution engine, basic monitoring, single-region deployment — this is a 68-point plan. As written, presenting 200+ connectors, ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, and multi-region HA as a coherent deliverable sequence, it scores 58: the core is buildable, but the scope promises will cause the project to stall at Step 14 and never recover.
